coredata.dc:title: Tungsten(VI) oxide as a versatile support for Pd nanocatalysts for alkaline direct alcohol fuel cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gwebu, Sandile S.
Selepe, Cyril T.
Matthews, Thabo
Zikhali, Memory
Mashola, Tebogo A.
Mbokazi, Siyabonga P.
Makhunga, Thobeka S.
Maxakato, Nobanathi W.
coredata.dc:description: The instability of carbon support materials has motivated the development of metal oxides supports which are stable under the fuel cell environment. In this study, tungsten (VI) oxide (WO3) is utilized as a secondary support and cocatalyst for the electrooxidation of methanol and ethanol. Functionalized carbon nanodots employed as primary supports were blended with WO3 nanoparticles to form a composite support onto which Pd nanoparticles were deposited by a borohydride reduction method. The synthesized Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts were characterized by Transmission Electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffractometry (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. XRD results proved that incorporating WO3 into Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst shifts the Pd diffraction peaks to lower 2Ɵ value due to lattice relaxation. XPS results revealed that W exist in oxidised form and confirmed the strong interaction between the support material and the catalyst. The Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts exhibited a remarkable catalytic activity towards methanol and ethanol oxidation. High current densities of 87.24 mA cm−2 and 44.23 mA cm−2 were obtained for ethanol and methanol oxidation, respectively, using a catalyst with 2.5% Pd loading. EIS, CA and stability tests revealed that the presence of WO3 in Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst improves the kinetics, tolerance to poisoning and long-term durability in alkaline conditions. This superior performance is attributed to the electronic coupling between Pd and WO3 nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tungsten trioxide
Palladium
Electrocatalyst
Methanol
Ethanol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies for ethanol and methanol electrooxidation reactions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the assistance and support provided by the Electrochemistry group , Centre for Nanomaterials Science Research, University of Johannesburg, South Africa , The University Research Council International Postgraduate Scholarships (2021–2023), Department of Chemical Sciences , University of Johannesburg , South Africa. The authors are grateful to the funding parties that have supported this research financially since its inception.
This research was funded by National Research Foundation of South Africa (grant numbers 112812 and NRF-TTK 118148 ), University Research Council, Department of Science and Technology .

This research was funded by National Research Foundation of South Africa (grant numbers 112812 and NRF-TTK 118148), University Research Council, Department of Science and Technology.The authors gratefully acknowledge the assistance and support provided by the Electrochemistry group, Centre for Nanomaterials Science Research, University of Johannesburg, South Africa, The University Research Council International Postgraduate Scholarships (2021–2023), Department of Chemical Sciences, University of Johannesburg, South Africa. The authors are grateful to the funding parties that have supported this research financially since its inception.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images for fCNDs-WO3 nanoparticles (a), Pd/fCNDs-WO3 (b) particle size histogram (c) and elemental maps for the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst (d).
XRD patterns of Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts.
XPS (a) survey spectra of fCNDs, fCNDs-WO3 and Pd/fCNDs-WO3, deconvoluted spectra W 4f of fCNDs-WO3 (b), Pd 3d of Pd/fCNDs-WO3 (c), W 4f of Pd/fCNDs-WO3 (d).
Cyclic voltammograms (a–c) for the electrooxidation of 1 M ethanol in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte on fCNDs-WO3, Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts.
Chronoamperometry curves (a) and stability curves (b) for the electrooxidation of 1 M ethanol in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte on fCNDs-WO3, Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts.
Cyclic voltammograms for the electrooxidation of 1 M methanol in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte on fCNDs-WO3, Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst.
Chronoamperometry curves (a) and durability curves (b) for the electrooxidation of 1 M methanol in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte on fCNDs-WO3, Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst.

EIS Nyquist plots for Pd/fCNDS and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst in 0.1 M KOH solutions containing 1 M ethanol (a) and 1 M methanol (b). (The frequencies used ranged from 100 kHz to 100 mKz at −0.4V). Equivalent circuit corresponding to the Nyquist plots (c).
Average crystal size of Pd crystal Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalysts.
Comparison of electrochemical performance of Pd/fCNDs-WO3 against other WO3-based anode catalysts for direct alcohol fuel cells.
Electrochemical impedance data observed for Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Tungsten(VI) oxide as a versatile support for Pd nanocatalysts for alkaline direct alcohol fuel cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences, University of Johannesburg, Doornfontein, Johannesburg 2028, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Sciences, University of Johannesburg, Doornfontein, Johannesburg 2028, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The instability of carbon support materials has motivated the development of metal oxides supports which are stable under the fuel cell environment. In this study, tungsten (VI) oxide (WO3) is utilized as a secondary support and cocatalyst for the electrooxidation of methanol and ethanol. Functionalized carbon nanodots employed as primary supports were blended with WO3 nanoparticles to form a composite support onto which Pd nanoparticles were deposited by a borohydride reduction method. The synthesized Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts were characterized by Transmission Electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffractometry (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. XRD results proved that incorporating WO3 into Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst shifts the Pd diffraction peaks to lower 2Ɵ value due to lattice relaxation. XPS results revealed that W exist in oxidised form and confirmed the strong interaction between the support material and the catalyst. The Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts exhibited a remarkable catalytic activity towards methanol and ethanol oxidation. High current densities of 87.24 mA cm−2 and 44.23 mA cm−2 were obtained for ethanol and methanol oxidation, respectively, using a catalyst with 2.5% Pd loading. EIS, CA and stability tests revealed that the presence of WO3 in Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst improves the kinetics, tolerance to poisoning and long-term durability in alkaline conditions. This superior performance is attributed to the electronic coupling between Pd and WO3 nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrooxidation of alcohol fuel cells has been investigated in both acidic and alkaline environments on both Pt/C and Pd/C state of art electrocatalysts, interestingly, high current densities have been obtained under alkaline environments [1–4]. Palladium is currently the most attractive electrocatalyst for direct alcohol fuel cells operating in alkaline conditions due to the high oxyphilic features and inertness on C–C bond cleavage. It is also cheaper and more abundant than platinum [5–12]. Even though, palladium-based catalysts have shown great catalytic activity towards methanol and ethanol oxidation they still suffer from poisoning and long-term stability [4,13–19]. Palladium is more abundant and its cost is less than that of platinum, however, it is still expensive for practical commercialisation of direct alcohol fuel cells. To overcome the cost, highly active catalysts with low Pd loading must be developed. Resistance to poisoning of Pd catalysts can be achieved by adding a cocatalyst or a promoter [20,21]. Design of active and durable fuel cell catalysts requires careful selection of appropriate support materials and cocatalysts/promoters. Carbon support materials possess appealing properties such as large surface area and great electrical conductivity. Nonetheless, most common carbon nanostructures suffer from corrosion under the harsh fuel cell environment.

Inorganic support materials such as TiO2 [22–24], RuO2 [25], MnO2 [26,27], V2O5 [25], SnO2 [20], Co3O4 [28], La2O3 [21], ZrO2 [29,30] and WO3 [31–33] have been explored as potential cocatalysts and support materials, it has been found that they serve multiple functions: as metal-free catalysts [34,35] promoters for metal nanocatalysts and as primary [36] or secondary support materials [37]. Of all the inorganic support materials, tungsten trioxide (WO3) has outstanding electrochemical and photocatalytic properties which enable its use in various applications [32,38,39]. Georgieva et al. [38] developed a Pt/WO3/Gr electrode which showed outstanding catalytic activity for methanol electrooxidation than a commercial Pt/C catalyst. The performance of the electrode could be further enhanced by irradiating it with visible light. They discovered that increased the catalytic activity is caused by simultaneous methanol photooxidation on WO3 sites and a synergistic effect between Pt and WO3 which enhances the electrooxidation of methanol, and adsorbed CO oxidation on Pt sites. Dias et al. [39] performed photoelectrochemical water splitting on tungsten trioxide photoanodes, they observed that photocurrent density increased with temperature and the onset potential shifted to lower potentials indicating enhanced electrocatalytic activity. However, the photoanodes suffered from poor stability after 72 h.

Carbon supported Pt–WO3 electrocatalysts have proven to be more electroactive than the commercial Pt/C electrocatalyst in a PEMFC testing station [31]. The morphology of WO3 nanoparticles also contribute to the activity of the electrocatalyst. Zhang et al. [33] observed that hexagonal tungsten trioxide microspheres with nest-like pores are more active than the commercial Pt/C and Pt/WO3 electrocatalysts towards methanol oxidation in acidic media due to large tunnels in the hexagonal structure which offers high porous surface area. WO3 is considered a suitable support material for fuel cells operating in acidic media because it can form an electron conducting hydrogen tungsten bronze compound (HxWO3) that facilitates the dehydrogenation of methanol molecules adsorbed on Pt surface. The spill over of the hydrogen fragments of dehydrogenated species from Pt onto the surface of the hydrogen tungsten bronze helps to free the Pt sites for further chemisorption of methanol molecules [33,38].

Pd-WO3 nanopartices supported on carbon black have been trialed as cathode electrocatalysts for DMFC and they have showed better activity than commercial Pd/C [40]. Despite the outstanding contribution of WO3 towards the electroactivity of Pd and/or Pt, until recently, Pd-WO3 anode catalysts for ADAFC had not been studied. We acknowledge the recent work of Wang et al [41] which reports a Pd-WO3 electrocatalysts supported on XC-72 for ethanol electrooxidation in alkaline media. First-principles calculations revealed that the electronic coupling interaction between Pd and WO3 nanoparticles accelerates ethanol electro-oxidation reactions.

The aim of this study was to understand the role of WO3 in Pd-based electrocatalyst for ADAFC. We developed a low Pd loading catalyst supported on functionalized carbon nanodots blended with WO3 nanoparticles. To clearly understand the contribution of WO3 in our catalyst, we also synthesized Pd/fCNDs electrocatalysts and fCNDs-WO3 composite for use as benchmark standards. CNT-derived carbon nanodots were chosen as primary support materials owing their large surface area and crystallinity. Physical and chemical properties of the prepared electrocatalyst were studied by TEM, XPS and XRD. The electrocatalytic activity of the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalyst towards methanol and ethanol oxidation was studied in alkaline electrolyte using KOH as supporting electrolyte.

Nafion solution 10 wt%, Absolute ethanol >99.9%, Methanol >99.9%, Anhydrous PdCl2, and KOH pellets were purchased from Merck and used as received without purification or modification. Monoclinic WO3 nanoparticles were obtained from the Photocatalysis lab (University of Johannesburg) and used without further purification [42].

Functionalized carbon nanodots (fCNDs) (4g) and WO3 nanoparticles (1g) were mechanically crushed together and dispersed in 50% aqueous ethanol solution. The black suspension was treated in an ultrasound bath for 1 h. The resulting black solids were recovered by filtration, dried in the oven at 60 °C for 12 h before they were calcined in a furnace at 300 °C for 2 h. The contents were cooled down at room temperature to obtain fCNDs-WO3 composite. Pd nanoparticles were deposited onto the composite support by a sonochemical-assisted borohydride reduction method described in our previous work [43].

Surface morphology of the material was examined using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) JEOL JEM 2100. XRD patterns were obtained from a Philips X'PertPro X-Ray Diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation with the following conditions: λ = 0.154443 nm, 40 kV and 40 mA. XPS spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific (ESCAlab 250Xi) equipped with a monochromatic Aluminium anode. The beam size and power were 900 μm and 300W, respectively.

Modified glass carbon working electrodes were prepared following method described in our previous work [43]. A Dropsens potentiostat (μSTAT8000, Switzerland) was used for cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry tests. The Dropsens potentiostat is only limited to voltammetry and chronoamperometry measurements, therefore, an Ivium compactstat was used for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy studies. The electrolytes were deaerated by purging in argon gas for 30 min before conducting the tests.

The shape and size of the fCNDs-WO3 and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 nanoparticles was investigated using a transmission microscope and the micrograph are shown in Fig. 1
. The TEM image of fCNDs-WO3 (Fig. 1a) shows that carbon nanodots are well blended with WO3 nanoparticles, however, the nanoparticles show some apparent agglomeration typical of metal oxides [42,44]. Due to the agglomerated nature of the composite support, the particle size could not be estimated. Fig. 1b shows the TEM micrograph of the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst. Pd nanoparticles are observed as small black spots on fCNDs-WO3 support. Such spots were not observed on the TEM micrograph of fCNDs-WO3 nanoparticles. Lattice fringes with spacing distances of 0.303 nm, 0.227 nm and 0.38 nm which correspond to C(002), Pd(111) and WO3, respectively, were also observed thus confirming successful preparation of Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst. An image taken at low resolution was used to estimate the particle sizes of Pd nanoparticles as presented in the histogram (Fig. 1c). As shown by the histogram, Pd particle sizes ranged between 3 and 11 nm.

It has been reported that inorganic support materials interact with catalysts nanoparticles thereby resulting in a geometric change in crystal structure [45]. To elucidate the effect of WO3 on the crystal structure of Pd, XRD was used to study the crystal structures of Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalysts (Fig. 2). The XRD pattern of Pd/fCNDs shows a broad diffraction peak at 2ϴ = 25° which corresponds to the C (002) facet arising from the fCNDs support. Peaks observed at 2ϴ = 40, 47, 68, 82.5 and 86° are attributed to Pd (111), Pd (200), Pd (220), Pd (311) and Pd (222) facets respectively. It is noticeable that these peaks shift to lower 2ϴ values upon addition of WO3 indicating lattice relaxation. Other studies on metal oxide supported catalysts nanoparticles have reported positive shifts (strained lattices) for (111), (200) and (220) facets of both Pd and Pt [25,44]. This unique lattice relaxation could be attributed to the interaction between WO3 and Pd. It is worth mentioning that the peak intensities of the Pd facets in Pd/fCNDs-WO3 is higher than those in Pd/fCNDs indicating that the metal nanoparticles deposited on fCNDs-WO3 exhibit outstanding crystallinity. A peak corresponding to C (002) at 2ϴ = 25° is also observed in the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 XRD pattern. Interestingly, the C(002) peak is not deformed and its intensity is the same as that of C(002) in the Pd/fCNDs pattern indicating that addition of WO3 did not diminish the crystallinity of fCNDs. Diffraction peaks 2ϴ = 43 and 52° were assigned to WO3 facets with orientations (222) and (024) respectively according to ICDD: 04-007-1277. The average crystal size of Pd in each catalyst was calculated from the Pd (111) facet using Debye-Scherrer equation. The results are presented in Table 1 .
The crystal size of Pd in Pd/fCNDs-WO3 is bigger than that of Pd in Pd/fCNDs. This could be attributed to agglomeration as often observed for metal oxide-based catalysts [36,44].

The composition and oxidation states of Pd in each catalyst was determined using XPS. Fig. 4a shows the survey spectra of fCNDs, fCNDs-WO3 and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 nanoparticles. The survey spectrum for fCNDs show major peaks for C 1s and O 1s. A small peak with binding energy corresponding to magnesium is also observed and could be an impurity arising from the synthesis and functionalisation of fCNDs. The C 1s and O 1s peaks are also present in the survey spectra of fCNDs-WO3 and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 although their intensity is reduced. As expected, the W 4f peak is present in the survey spectra of both fCNDs-WO3 and Pd/fCNDs-WO3. The survey spectrum of Pd/fCNDs-WO3 shows a peak which corresponds to Pd 3d thus confirming the presence of Pd in the electrocatalyst. The Pd 3d and W 4f peaks were deconvoluted to reveal the oxidation states of W and Pd in the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalyst.

The W 4f scan for both fCNDs-WO3 and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 (Figure b and c) consists of two peaks which are ascribed to W 4f 7/2 and W 4f 5/2 of WO3. Peaks corresponding to the binding energy of metallic W were not present implying that W exists in oxidised form in the catalyst. Worth note, the binding energies of W 4f 7/2 and W 4f 5/2 in fCNDs-WO3 are 35.4 eV and 37.6 eV, respectively. In contrast, these peaks appear at 35.7 eV and 37.9 eV in the deconvoluted W 4f spectrum of Pd/fCNDs-WO3. This positive shift is attributed to the interaction between WO3 and Pd catalyst [46].

The deconvoluted spectrum of Pd 3d gave four peaks which correspond to Pd (metal) (BE = 335.3 eV and 340.5 eV) and an oxidised form PdO with binding energies of 337.4 and 342.6 eV. These values agree with those reported in literature [47,48]. Functionalized CNDs posses –CO and -C-O functional groups [49]. The XPS results also confirm that tungsten (W) is neither reduced nor oxidised in the catalysts and exists in oxidised form (WO3). The presence of PdO could possibly mean that palladium is anchored onto fCNDs through C–O and –CO moties and onto WO3 through -O-WO2 group.

XPS was also used to quantify the elemental composition and of interest the Pd content in the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalyst. Table 2 summarises the elemental composition of the elements present in the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalyst.

The Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalyst was mainly composed of C, O, W and Pd. Some trace elements such as Mg, Al and Na were also detected. These elements could be arising from the materials used to prepare the catalyst. The Pd loading was found to be 2.5%, however, although a 5% Pd loading was targeted. Noteworthy, the Pd and PdO coexist in the electrocatalysts (Fig. 3 c). It has been reported that PdO species are more tolerant to poisoning and play a vital role in oxidising CO-like poisoning species during alcohol electrooxidation reactions [50,51]. Having a mixture of Pd and PdO could help improve the overall catalyst performance.

It is important to determine the electrochemically active surface area (EASA) of the electrode materials before studying the alcohol oxidation reactions, this helps to evaluate the surface area that is accessible to the electrolyte for charge transfer. Cyclic voltammograms shown in Fig. 4a were recorded in 0.1 M KOH at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1 at room temperature to determine the EASA of the prepared electrocatalysts. The CV curve for fCNDs-WO3 does not show any peak for both cathodic and anodic scans possibly due to poor adsorption/desorption of hydroxyl ions. The forward scan for the Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst does not show any peak. This is not surprising as it has been reported that bulk palladium electrodes are unable to absorb hydrogen. Because of that, it is difficult to estimate the charge of a monolayer of adsorbed hydrogen on palladium electrodes [52]. As shown in Fig. 4a, the anodic scan for Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst show a peak at −0.09V is attributed to the adsorption/desorption of hydroxyl ions. The cathodic scans for both Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts show broad peaks at −0.92V and −0.66V, respectively. These peaks are attributed to the reduction of palladium oxide and were integrated to find the charge of the monolayer of chemisorbed oxygen. The PdO reduction peaks for carbon supported Pd nanoparticles has been observed at ∼0.45V [53,54], interestingly, for the Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst the peak appears at a low potential of −0.66V possibly due to the fCNDs support with improved surface area. Addition of WO3 further shifts the PdO reduction peak to −0.92V indicating improved electroactivity. This result implies that WO3 is acting as co-catalyst. The EASA of the electrode materials were calculated using the formula:
E A S A = Q / s l
where Q is coulombic charge obtained by integrating the area under the PdO reduction peak, s is the proportionality constant (405
μ
C cm−2) and l is the palladium loading (g cm−2). As mentioned above, the Pd loading on Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts was 10% and 2.5% respectively. Using these Pd loadings, the EASA for the Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts was 103.7 cm2 mg−1 and 385.15 cm2 mg 1, respectively.

The electrocatalytic performance of the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 of the synthesized electrocatalyst was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry in 1 M ethanol in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The recorded cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 4c) for both Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts show two anodic peaks in the forward and reverse scans. These peaks observed in the forward scan infer the electrooxidation of ethanol while the reverse scan peak corresponds to the oxidation of carbonaceous species produced in the forward scan. A good anode catalyst should yield a high current density in the forward scan. Fig. 4b show that the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts yielded a high current of 87.24 mA cm−2, almost twice higher than that of the Pd/fCNDs (44.46 mA cm−2). No significant peaks were observed for the fCNDs-WO3 composite implying that WO3 has little or no ability to oxidise ethanol. The ratio of the current produced in the forward scan (If) to the current produced in the reverse scan (Ib) is used to measure the degree of oxophilicity, which is the catalysts’ capability to withstand CO poisoning. The If/Ib for the Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst was found to be 1.04. The WO3-modified catalyst (Pd/fCNDs-WO3) had a remarkable If/Ib ratio of 1.62 indicating improved tolerance to poisoning. The onset potential of the forward scan is another measure used to evaluate the catalytic activity of a material. A good catalyst should catalyse alcohol fuels at low onset potentials. Interestingly, the onset potential of the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalyst was observed at −0.49 V whereas that of Pd/fCNDs appeared at −0.35 V. The low onset potentials observed for the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalyst is brought by WO3 nanoparticles acting as cocatalyst.

To obtain a fair comparison, the current densities were normalized taking into account the mass of Pd in each catalyst. A shown in Fig. 4c the mass activity of the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst is estimated to be 488 mA mg−1 Pd. This is ∼7.8 times higher than that of the Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst indicating that the presence of WO3 significantly enhances the performance of Pd nanocatalyst. As reported with other metal oxides, it is likely that WO3 provides high concentrations of the OH− ions towards ethanol oxidation. Also, the presence of WO3 in the nanostructure of fCNDs improves the distribution of Pd nanoparticles thus increasing the electrochemically active surface area [53].

Chronoamperometry is a reliable technique for investigating the electrode kinetics diffusion and adsorption processes. It was employed to study the catalysts’ capabilities of oxidising poisoning species. Chronoamprograms (Fig. 5 a) for the synthesized catalysts were recorded in 1 M ethanol +0.1 M KOH electrolyte at a fixed voltage the corresponding to the oxidation potential of ethanol. This value is obtained from the forward peak of the cyclic voltammograms. From the chronoamprograms, both catalysts show a huge current decrease at the start of the reaction, this is more visible on Pd/fCNDs curve. This sharp decrease is attributed to the accumulation of CO-like intermediates and the formation of Pd–OH or Pd–O species produced during alcohol oxidation [53]. Both electrocatalysts reached pseudo state almost at the same time (∼500 s). As the reaction progressed both electrocatalysts were capable of oxidising ethanol for a period of 72000 s. Interestingly, the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst retained the highest current density for the entire period of 72000 s. This can be explained by the fact that Pd/fCNDs-WO3 exhibits anti-poisoning properties and large EASA for enhanced kinetics.

To further investigate the durability and long-term stability, 1000 consecutive cyclic voltammograms were performed in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte containing 1 M ethanol at a low scan rate of 10 mV s−1. Fig. 5b shows the changes in ethanol oxidation peak current densities obtained during successive CV cycles. The peak current densities of both Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts increases as the cycle number increases from 1 to 100. This growth in current density could be attributed to the activation of the electrocatalyst surface [53]. However, beyond the 100th cycle a drop in current density is observed. This could be due to accumulation of poisoning species which then obstruct fresh ethanol molecules from adsorbing on the surface of the electrodes. For the Pd/fCNDs catalyst a continuous drop in current is observed between the 200th and the 700th cycle. This catalyst shows poor stability and durability because after the 700th cycle a steady state is achieved but it is accompanied by a 82% decline from the highest peak current density. After 500 cycles the curve for Pd/fCNDs-WO3 lies flat and horizontal with a current decline of 33.65%. This result implies that WO3 play a role in improving the durability and long-term stability of Pd-based DEFC electrodes.

Methanol electrooxidation was investigated in the presence of a 0.1 M KOH supporting electrolyte at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1, Fig. 6 . The CV curve recorded for the fCNDs-WO3 nanocomposite do not show any peaks on both anodic and cathodic scans indicating that the fCNDs-WO3 is incapable of cleaving the methanol molecule. Methanol oxidation on the Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst was characterized by typical CV curves for MOR showing distinct peaks on both forward and reverse scans. The current density obtained using the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 is four times higher than that obtained using Pd/fCNDs. Worth note, the onset potential for the electroactivity of Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst is −0.41V, thus 0.31V less than that of the Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst. The If/Ib ratio observed for the Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst is 5.04, however, for the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 ratio is significantly greater implying that WO3 improves palladium's tolerance to poisoning.

Fig. 7 a shows the chronoamperometry curves observed for 1 M methanol electrooxidation on fCNDs-WO3, Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst. The working potentials were fixed at 0.1V and −0.2V for Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts, respectively. At the start of the reaction, a sharp current drop was observed due to formation of CO-like and OH- species adsorbing on the surface of the Pd catalyst. As the reaction progressed both curves became horizontally flat indicating that both electrocatalysts are capable of oxidising poisoning species. As shown in Fig. 7a, the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst reached at pseudo steady quicker (at 800 s) and retained a higher current density than the Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst for the entire test period of 72000 s. This superior performance of the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst is attributed to the presence of WO3 cocatalyst/support which helps to oxidise CO-like poisoning species.

Durability and long-term stability of the electrocatalyst was also tested in the presence of methanol in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte at 10 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 7b, both catalysts are stable up to 100 cycles as indicated by growths in current density from the 1st to the 100th cycle. A gradual decrease between the 150th and 600th cycle is observed for the Pd/fCNDs due to the continuous formation of CO intermediates which adsorb on the surface of the catalyst. However, the current drop for the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 (between 200th-600th) is small and no further decrease is observed after the 600th cycle. The improved stability is linked to the presence of a ceramic support material (WO3) which helps to improve the durability and poisoning tolerance of the electrocatalyst.

The electroactivity of the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 was compared to that of other WO3-based catalysts and the results are summarized in Table 3 . Until to date, Pd-WO3 based nanocatalysts for ADAFC have not been reported. As shown in Table, the synthesized Pd/fCNDs-WO3 exhibits lower onset potentials, greater mass activity and current densities compared to Pd-WO3/C, Pt-WO3 and Pt/C-WO3 DAFC electrocatalysts reported in literature. These results imply that achoring Pd onto WO3 improves the electrocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the low Pd content in Pd/fCNDs-WO3 catalyst (2.5 wt%) makes this material a promising anode electrode considering the high cost of Pd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies for ethanol and methanol electrooxidation reactions
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is a useful technique for investigating electrooxidation kinetics of alcohol fuel cells. Fig. 8 shows the Nyquist plots and the Randles equivalent circuit obtained from fitting impedance data on Ivium software. As seen in Fig. 8a and b, at low and high frequencies the Nyquist plots appear as capacitive semicircles on the positive side on the plot. Worth note, a bigger diameter is observed for the Pd/fCNDs indicating higher resistance and low electrode kinetics. In both methanol and ethanol, no impedance behaviours were observed in the negative region of Nyquist plot. This is an indication that both the Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts exhibit good tolerance to poisoning. Negative impedance in alcohol fuel cells is due to electrooxidation of CO-like intermediates formed during AORs [53]. In EIS, the equivalent circuit model which fits the data obtained helps to understand the chemical process occurring at the surface of the electrode. As shown in Fig. 8c, the equivalent circuit accounted for the solution resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct) and the double layer capacitance (CPE). The magnitude of these parameters depends on the electrode potential, catalyst surface and the kinetics of the reaction.

The impedance components for the Pd/fCNDs and Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts in both ethanol and methanol are summarized in Table 4 . The magnitude of the charge transfer resistance (Rct) corresponds to the diameter of the Nyquist plot semicircle and is used to measure electro-transfer kinetics. A lower Rct value (smaller diameter of a semicircle) is an indication of faster electro-transfer kinetics.

As shown in Table 4, the Rct value for the Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst is lower than that of the Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst in both alcohol fuel signifying that addition of WO3 into Pd/fCNDs improves electrooxidation kinetics. It is worth mentioning that the charge transfer resistances for methanol are lower those for ethanol because the methanol molecule is smaller and easier to oxidise.

Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalyst was synthesized by a borohydride reduction method and its electrocatalytic activity towards methanol and ethanol was investigated in alkaline media to understand the role of WO3. XRD and XPS were used to study the influence of WO3 on the structural characteristics of Pd. Incorporating WO3 into Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst shifts the Pd diffraction peaks to lower 2Ɵ value due to lattice relaxation. XPS results revealed that W exist in oxidised form and confirmed the strong interaction between the support material and the catalyst. No catalytic activity was observed the for fCNDs-WO3 implying WO3 alone does not catalyse the electrooxidation of ethanol, therefore, it is acting as a support material. The Pd/fCNDs-WO3 electrocatalysts displayed a remarkable catalytic activity towards methanol and ethanol oxidation, ∼7.8 times higher than that of the Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst demonstrating that WO3 is a cocatalyst, i.e; it enhances the performance of Pd nanocatalyst. High current densities of 87.24 mA cm−2 and 44.23 mA cm−2 were obtained for ethanol and methanol oxidation, respectively. EIS, CA and stability tests revealed that the presence of WO3 in Pd/fCNDs electrocatalyst improves the kinetics, tolerance to poisoning and long-term durability in alkaline conditions. This superior performance is attributed to the contribution of WO3 nanoparticles via electronic coupling interaction with Pd. We conclude that in this model catalyst WO3 is a versatile material: serves multiple purposes of supporting Pd nanoparticles, cocatalysing alcohol fuels and improving the durability of the electrode.

Sandile S. Gwebu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing—original draft preparation, Writing—review and editing, Project administration. Cyril T. Selepe: Validation, Visualization. Thabo Matthews: Software, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Memory Zikhali: Writing—reviewing and editing. Tebogo A. Mashola: Data curation. Siyabonga P. Mbokazi: Writing—reviewing and editing. Thobeka S. Makhunga: Investigation. Nobanathi W. Maxakato: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.
This research was funded by National Research Foundation of South Africa (grant numbers 112812 and NRF-TTK 118148), University Research Council, Department of Science and Technology.
Not applicable.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the assistance and support provided by the Electrochemistry group, Centre for Nanomaterials Science Research, University of Johannesburg, South Africa, The University Research Council International Postgraduate Scholarships (2021–2023), Department of Chemical Sciences, University of Johannesburg, South Africa. The authors are grateful to the funding parties that have supported this research financially since its inception.
